perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to main tain the confidentiality of such Pr oprietary Inform ation and to not use, disclose, publish or dissem inate it in an y manner not consiste nt with this Agreement. In additi on to any other remedies, which may be available, Owner shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the ter ms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to maintain the confidentiality of such Proprietary Information and to not use, disclose, publish, or disseminate it in any manner that is reasonably inconsistent with this Agreement. In addition to any other remedies which may be available, Owner may be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'reasonably inconsistent' introduces ambiguity, making it unclear what constitutes a breach of confidentiality. Changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may be entitled' weakens the guarantee of injunctive relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the protection of trade secrets by introducing the term 'reasonably inconsistent,' which provides a loophole for potential misuse of proprietary information. The original text clearly states 'not consistent' requiring strict adherence, while the modified version allows for subjective interpretation. Under California law (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2), injunctive relief is available for actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets, meaning the original 'shall be entitled' is more in line with ensuring a remedy for the trade secret holder.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'not consistent' to 'reasonably inconsistent,' weakening the protection of proprietary information. This contradicts the intent of Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2, which allows injunctive relief for 'actual or threatened misappropriation.' The original text's 'shall be entitled' aligns better with ensuring a remedy, while 'may be entitled' weakens this guarantee, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the certainty of injunctive relief for trade secret violations.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient cer tifies that no Proprietary Inform ation, or any part of it will be exported to any countr y in violation of the export laws and regulations of the United States government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient understands that Proprietary Information, or any part of it, may be subject to export laws and regulations of the United States government.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'certifies that...will be' to 'understands that...may be' removes a clear certification of compliance with export laws and replaces it with a mere acknowledgement that such laws may apply. It weakens the legal obligation and removes the guarantee of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text includes a certification that proprietary information will not be exported in violation of U.S. export laws. By changing this to a mere understanding that the information *may* be subject to those laws, the modified text eliminates the binding promise of compliance and creates ambiguity about the recipient's actual commitment to adhering to export regulations. This contradicts the spirit and intent of the EAR, which requires active compliance and responsibility on the part of exporters (or recipients of exported information) to ensure adherence to export controls.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text contained a certification of compliance with export laws, a strong commitment. The modified text weakens this to a mere acknowledgement that export laws *may* apply. This undermines the legal intent of ensuring compliance with export regulations, as described in the Law Explanation, and introduces ambiguity regarding the recipient's commitment. Although the snippets don't directly say that a certification is required, the change from a guarantee of compliance to a simple understanding of possible regulations introduces an unacceptable level of ambiguity, creating a potential non-compliance scenario that contradicts the original intent.
